blocks:
Ayahs

ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ (١)
The All-beneficent (1)
عَلَّمَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ (٢)
has taught the Quran. (2)
خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ (٣)
He created man, (3)
عَلَّمَهُ ٱلْبَيَانَ (٤)
[and] taught him articulate speech. (4)
ٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ بِحُسْبَانٍ (٥)
The sun and the moon are [disposed] calculatedly, (5)
وَٱلنَّجْمُ وَٱلشَّجَرُ يَسْجُدَانِ (٦)
and the herb and the tree prostrate [to Allah]. (6)
وَٱلسَّمَآءَ رَفَعَهَا وَوَضَعَ ٱلْمِيزَانَ (٧)
He raised the heaven high and set up the balance, (7)
أَلَّا تَطْغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْمِيزَانِ (٨)
declaring, ‘Do not infringe the balance! (8)
وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلْوَزْنَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ وَلَا تُخْسِرُوا۟ ٱلْمِيزَانَ (٩)
Maintain the weights with justice, and do not shorten the balance!’ (9)
وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَضَعَهَا لِلْأَنَامِ (١٠)
And the earth—He laid it out for mankind. (10)
فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ وَٱلنَّخْلُ ذَاتُ ٱلْأَكْمَامِ (١١)
In it are fruits and date-palms with sheaths, (11)
وَٱلْحَبُّ ذُو ٱلْعَصْفِ وَٱلرَّيْحَانُ (١٢)
grain with husk, and fragrant herbs. (12)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (١٣)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (13)
خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن صَلْصَـٰلٍ كَٱلْفَخَّارِ (١٤)
He created man out of dry clay, like the potter’s, (14)
وَخَلَقَ ٱلْجَآنَّ مِن مَّارِجٍ مِّن نَّارٍ (١٥)
and created the jinn out of a flame of a fire. (15)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (١٦)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (16)
رَبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقَيْنِ وَرَبُّ ٱلْمَغْرِبَيْنِ (١٧)
Lord of the two easts, and Lord of the two wests! (17)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (١٨)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (18)
مَرَجَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ يَلْتَقِيَانِ (١٩)
He merged the two seas, meeting each other. (19)
بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخٌ لَّا يَبْغِيَانِ (٢٠)
There is a barrier between them, which they do not overstep. (20)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٢١)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (21)
يَخْرُجُ مِنْهُمَا ٱللُّؤْلُؤُ وَٱلْمَرْجَانُ (٢٢)
From them emerge the pearl and the coral. (22)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٢٣)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (23)
وَلَهُ ٱلْجَوَارِ ٱلْمُنشَـَٔاتُ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ كَٱلْأَعْلَـٰمِ (٢٤)
His are the sailing ships on the sea [appearing] like landmarks. (24)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٢٥)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (25)
كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍ (٢٦)
Everyone on it is ephemeral, (26)
وَيَبْقَىٰ وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو ٱلْجَلَـٰلِ وَٱلْإِكْرَامِ (٢٧)
yet lasting is the majestic and munificent Face of your Lord. (27)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٢٨)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (28)
يَسْـَٔلُهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِى شَأْنٍ (٢٩)
Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him. Every day He is engaged in some work. (29)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٠)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (30)

(Commentary)

The Surah includes references to His creation, the Exalted, of the world with its constituent parts—the heavens, the earth, the land, and the sea, as well as mankind and the jinn. It describes the ordering of its parts in a system that benefits the two weighty races (الثقلان)—mankind (الإنس) and the jinn (الجن)—in their lives. Through this, the world is divided into two stages of existence (نشأتين): the worldly life (نشأة دنيا), which will perish with the perishing of its inhabitants, and another life (نشأة أخرى) that is everlasting, wherein felicity is distinguished from wretchedness, and blessing from vengeance.

From this, it becomes evident that the realm of existence (دار الوجود), in both its worldly and otherworldly aspects, possesses a single system whose parts are harmonious, whose portions are interconnected, and whose pillars are firm; one part is rectified by another, and one half is completed by the other.

Thus, whatever exists therein of essence or trace is among His blessings (نعمه) and bounties (آلائه). For this reason, He questions them time and again with an inquiry tinged with reproach, saying: فَبِأَيِّ آلَاءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ — "So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny?" This verse is repeated thirty-one times in the Surah.

Consequently, the Surah opens with the mention of the Exalted by His attribute of general mercy (رحمته العامة), which encompasses both the believer and the disbeliever, and the world and the Hereafter. It concludes with praise for Him in His saying: تَبَارَكَ اسْمُ رَبِّكَ ذِي الْجَلَالِ وَالْإِكْرَامِ — "Blessed is the Name of your Lord, the Possessor of Majesty and Generosity."

The Surah is potentially Meccan or Medinan, though its style (سياقها) more closely resembles the Meccan style. It is the only Surah in the Quran that opens, after the Basmalah, with one of the names of Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه). In al-Majma‘, narrated from Musa ibn Ja‘far, from his forefathers (peace be upon them), from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "Everything has a bride, and the bride of the Quran is Surah al-Rahman, majestic is His mention (جل ذكره)." This is also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Bayhaqi, from Ali (peace be upon him), from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

His saying, the Exalted: الرَّحْمَنُ عَلَّمَ الْقُرْآنَ — "The All-Beneficent has taught the Quran." The All-Beneficent (الرحمن) — the attribute of Allah denoting His expansive mercy that encompasses all creation in this world — is, as previously mentioned in the commentary on Surah al-Fatihah, an intensive form (صيغة مبالغة) indicating the abundance of mercy through the bestowing of blessings. Therefore, it is appropriate that it encompasses the worldly blessings attained by both the believer and the disbeliever, as well as the otherworldly blessings attained by the believer. Due to its universality, it was fitting to begin the discourse with it, as the discourse in the Surah covers various types of worldly and otherworldly blessings by which the world of the two weighty races, mankind and jinn, is ordered.

Scholars have mentioned that al-Rahman is among the names exclusive to Him, the Exalted; no one else is named by it, unlike names such as al-Rahim (the Most Merciful) or al-Rahim (the Merciful).

His saying: عَلَّمَ الْقُرْآنَ — "has taught the Quran" is the commencement of enumerating the divine blessings. Since the Quran is the greatest of blessings in value and status, and the highest in station—because it is the speech of Allah that delineates His straight path (صراطه المستقيم) and includes the explanation of the way of felicity, which is the ultimate goal of every hoper and the end of every seeker's request—He prioritized the mention of its teaching over all other blessings, even over the creation of mankind and jinn, for whose instruction the Quran was revealed.

The first object (مفعول) of "taught" (عَلَّمَ) is omitted, which is "man" or "mankind and jinn." The implied meaning is: "He taught man the Quran" or "He taught mankind and jinn the Quran." Although scholars have not explicitly addressed this second possibility, it is the more likely of the two, because the Surah addresses the jinn alongside mankind throughout its verses. Had the teaching in His saying "taught the Quran" not included them, this address would not be complete.

It has also been said that the omitted object is Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or Gabriel, but what was mentioned previously is more suited to the context.

His saying, the Exalted: خَلَقَ الْإِنْسَانَ عَلَّمَهُ الْبَيَانَ — "He created man, and taught him articulate speech." He mentions the creation of man, and He will later mention the specific nature of his creation in His saying: خَلَقَ الْإِنْسَانَ مِنْ صَلْصَالٍ كَالْفَخَّارِ — "He created man out of dry clay like pottery." Man is among the most wondrous of Allah's creations, or rather the most wondrous of them; this becomes apparent by comparing his existence to that of other creatures and by reflecting upon the path of perfection (طريق الكمال) delineated for him in his outward and inward, and his world and his Hereafter. The Exalted said: لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسَانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَاهُ أَسْفَلَ سَافِلِينَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ — "We certainly created man in the best of forms; then We relegated him to the lowest of the low, except those who have faith and do righteous deeds" (95:4-6).

His saying: عَلَّمَهُ الْبَيَانَ — "taught him articulate speech." Articulate speech (البيان) — the capacity to disclose and clarify meaning through language — refers to speech that reveals what is in the mind. It is among the most wondrous of blessings, and His teaching it to man is part of the great divine providence (العناية الإلهية) concerning him. Speech is not merely the production of a sound using the lungs, the trachea, and the larynx, nor is it just the variation in the sound exiting the larynx by relying on the different points of articulation (مخارج الحروف) in the mouth.

Rather, through an inward inspiration (إلهام باطني) from Allah, the Glorified, man makes one of these sounds—relying on a point of articulation in the mouth called a "letter" (حرف), or a combination of several letters—a sign (علامة) pointing to a concept (مفهوم). Through this, he represents what is absent from the listener's senses and perception. He is thus able to bring forth any state of the witnessed world, whether it be grand or minute, existent or non-existent, past or future. Furthermore, he can bring forth any state of non-sensory meanings (المعاني غير المحسوسة) that man attains through thought and which the senses cannot access; he presents them all to his listener and represents them to his senses as if he were individuating them for him in their very essences.

Man's social civilization (اجتماعه المدني) would not be complete, nor would he have made this brilliant progress in his life, were it not for his awareness of the convention of speech (وضع الكلام) and his opening thereby the door of mutual understanding (التفهيم والتفهم). Were it not for that, he and the mute animals would be equal in the stagnation and inertia of life.

Among the strongest proofs that man's guidance to articulate speech is through a divine inspiration (إلهام إلهي) having an origin in creation (التكوين) is the diversity of languages (اختلاف اللغات) corresponding to the diversity of nations and groups in their spiritual characteristics and psychological traits, and according to the diversity of the natural regions in which they live. The Exalted said: وَمِنْ آيَاتِهِ خَلْقُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَاخْتِلَافُ أَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَأَلْوَانِكُمْ — "Among His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth, and the difference of your languages and colours" (30:22).

The meaning of His saying "taught him articulate speech" is not that Allah, the Glorified, established the languages and then taught them to man through revelation to one of the prophets or through inspiration. For man, by being placed in a social context, is naturally driven to adopt mutual understanding through signs and sounds—which is speech and logic—as social civilization cannot be completed without it.

Furthermore, His act, the Exalted, is creation and origination (التكوين والإيجاد), whereas the link between a word and its linguistic meaning is a conventional, mental construct (وضعية اعتبارية), not an external reality (حقيقية خارجية). Rather, Allah, the Glorified, created man and fashioned him with a primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — that leads him to social civilization, then to the establishment of language by making the word a sign for the meaning, such that when the word is delivered to the listener, it is as if the meaning itself is delivered. Then he was led to the establishment of writing (وضع الخط) by making specific shapes signs for words; thus, writing completes the purpose of speech, representing speech just as speech represents meaning.

In short, articulate speech is among the greatest of divine blessings and bounties (الآلاء) that preserve for the human species its human station and guide it toward every good.

This is what is apparent and immediately understood from the two verses. Regarding their meaning, there are other opinions: it is said that "man" refers to Adam (peace be upon him) and "articulate speech" refers to the names that Allah taught him. It is said that "man" is Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and "articulate speech" is the Quran or his teaching the believers the Quran. It is said that "articulate speech" refers to good and evil, which He taught to man. It is said it refers to the path of guidance and the path of misguidance, among other views, but these are far-fetched interpretations.

His saying, the Exalted: الشَّمْسُ وَالْقَمَرُ بِحُسْبَانٍ — "The sun and the moon [run] by a [precise] reckoning." Reckoning (الحسبان) is a verbal noun meaning calculation (الحساب). "The sun" is the subject (مبتدأ), "the moon" is conjoined to it, and "by a reckoning" is its predicate (خبر). The sentence is a predicate after a predicate for His saying "The All-Beneficent" (الرحمن). The implied meaning is: the sun and the moon run according to a calculation from Him, based on the type of motion He has decreed for them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالنَّجْمُ وَالشَّجَرُ يَسْجُدَانِ — "And the herb and the tree prostrate." They said: what is meant by the herb (النجم) is the vegetation that sprouts and emerges from the earth without a trunk, and the tree (الشجر) is the vegetation that possesses a trunk. This is a fine meaning supported by the grouping and pairing of the herb and the tree, even if the prior mention of the sun and the moon might suggest that "the star" (النجم) refers to the celestial bodies.

The prostration (سجود) of the herb and the tree is their submission (انقيادهما) to the divine command of origination and growth according to what has been decreed for them, as has been said. More precisely, they strike into the soil with their roots and fibers to absorb the elemental materials they need for nourishment. This falling upon the earth, manifesting their need for the Originator who fulfills their need—who is in reality Allah who nurtures them thus—is a prostration from them to Him, the Exalted.

The discussion regarding the grammatical position of His saying وَالنَّجْمُ وَالشَّجَرُ يَسْجُدَانِ is that it is conjoined to the previous verse, similar to the discussion on الشَّمْسُ وَالْقَمَرُ بِحُسْبَانٍ. The implied meaning is: and the herb and the tree prostrate to Him.

The author of al-Kashshaf said: "If you ask: How are these two sentences connected to 'The All-Beneficent'—meaning His saying 'The sun and the moon...' until His saying '...prostrate'? I say: They dispense with a verbal connection in favor of a semantic connection (الوصل المعنوي), as it is known that the reckoning is His reckoning and the prostration is to Him and none other."

Regarding the reason for leaving the previous verses—خَلَقَ الْإِنْسَانَ عَلَّمَهُ الْبَيَانَ الشَّمْسُ وَالْقَمَرُ بِحُسْبَانٍ—without a conjunction (العاطف), he stated in essence that these initial sentences are presented in the manner of enumeration (التعديد), so that each sentence is independent in rebuking those who denied the All-Beneficent and His bounties. This is like one who rebukes a denier of a benefactor's favors by enumerating them to him, saying: "Zayd enriched you after poverty, honored you after humiliation, increased you after fewness, did for you what no one has done for another; so what do you deny of his kindness?"

Then the discourse returns to its regular path after the rebuke, by connecting what ought to be connected due to harmony and proximity with a conjunction, so it is said: وَالنَّجْمُ وَالشَّجَرُ يَسْجُدَانِ وَالسَّمَاءَ رَفَعَهَا etc. (End of quote).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالسَّمَاءَ رَفَعَهَا وَوَضَعَ الْمِيزَانَ — "He raised the heaven and set up the balance." If "the heaven" (السماء) refers to the direction of height, then its raising means He created it in a raised state, not that He raised it after its creation. If it refers to the celestial bodies in the direction of height, then its raising is the decreeing of their positions such that they are raised relative to the earth by "unstitiching" (الفتق) after "stitching" (الرتق), as the Exalted said: أَوَلَمْ يَرَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ كَانَتَا رَتْقًا فَفَتَقْنَاهُمَا — "Have the faithless not regarded that the heavens and the earth were a solid mass and We clove them asunder?" (21:30). In any case, the raising is a sensory raising (رفع حسي).

If what is meant includes the stations of the noble angels and the sources of divine command and revelation, then the raising is metaphorical (معنوي), or it includes both the sensory and the metaphorical.

His saying: وَوَضَعَ الْمِيزَانَ — "and set up the balance." What is meant by the balance (الميزان) is everything by which a thing is weighed, i.e., measured, whether it be a belief, a word, or a deed. Among its instances is the scale used to weigh physical weights. The Exalted said: لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ وَأَنْزَلْنَا مَعَهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْمِيزَانَ لِيَقُومَ النَّاسُ بِالْقِسْطِ — "Certainly We sent Our apostles with manifest proofs, and We sent down with them the Book and the Balance, so that mankind may maintain justice" (57:25).

Thus, its apparent meaning is everything by which truth is distinguished from falsehood, sincerity from lying, justice from injustice, and virtue from vice, as it is the role of the Messenger to bring such from his Lord.

It has been said that the balance refers to justice (العدل), meaning: Allah established justice among you so that you may equalize things by giving every rightful owner their due.

It has also been said that it refers to the scale used for weighing physical weights. The first meaning is broader and more comprehensive.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَّا تَطْغَوْا فِي الْمِيزَانِ وَأَقِيمُوا الْوَزْنَ بِالْقِسْطِ وَلَا تُخْسِرُوا الْمِيزَانَ — "That you may not exceed the balance. Maintain the weights with justice, and do not stint the balance." It appears that what is meant by the balance here is the well-known scale, namely the scale for weights. Thus, His saying "That you may not exceed..." etc., is based on the assumption that the balance in the previous verse also refers to the scale for weights, and this is an explanation of the setting up of the balance. The meaning is: the purpose of Our setting up the balance among you is that you should be just in weighing and not transgress therein.

On the assumption that it refers to absolute right measurement or justice, it is the derivation of a particular ruling (حكم جزئي) from a universal ruling (حكم كلي). The meaning is: the necessary consequence of what We have established of right measurement or justice among you is that you weigh physical weights with equity and do not transgress therein.

In any case, it appears that the particle an (أَنْ) in His saying أَلَّا تَطْغَوْا is explanatory (تفسيرية). "Do not exceed" (لا تطغوا) is a prohibition against transgression in the balance, and "Maintain the weights with justice" is a command conjoined to it. Equity (القسط) is justice. "Do not stint the balance" (لَا تُخْسِرُوا الْمِيزَانَ) is another prohibition clarifying His saying "That you may not exceed..." and emphasizing it. Stinting (الإخسار) in the balance is to give short measure by increase or decrease such that the seller or the buyer suffers loss.

As for making "the balance" (الْمِيزَانَ) the governor and "that you may not exceed" (أَلَّا تَطْغَوْا) a negation—with the implied meaning "lest you exceed"—this would require a strained justification for conjoining an imperative (إنشاء) to a declarative (إخبار) in His saying "Maintain the weights..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْأَرْضَ وَضَعَهَا لِلْأَنَامِ — "And the earth, He set it for the creatures." The creatures (الأنام) refers to mankind; it has also been said to mean mankind and jinn, or everything that crawls upon the earth. In the expression regarding the earth as "setting" (الوضع) in contrast to the expression regarding the heaven as "raising" (الرفع), there is a manifest elegance.

His saying, the Exalted: فِيهَا فَاكِهَةٌ وَالنَّخْلُ ذَاتُ الْأَكْمَامِ — "In it are fruits and date-palms with sheaths." Fruit (الفاكهة) refers to fruits other than dates. Sheaths (الأكمام) is the plural of kumm (with damma or kasra on the kaf), which is the covering of the dates, namely the spathe (الطلع). As for the sleeve of a shirt (kumm), it is with damma on the kaf only, as has been said.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْحَبُّ ذُو الْعَصْفِ وَالرَّيْحَانُ — "And grain with its husk, and fragrance." This is conjoined to His saying "fruits," meaning: and in it are grain and fragrance. Grain (الحب) refers to that which is used for sustenance, such as wheat, barley, and rice. Husk (العصف) is that which serves as a covering for the grain, namely its chaff; it has also been interpreted as the leaves of the crop generally, or specifically the dry leaves. Fragrance (الريحان) refers to sweet-smelling plants.

His saying, the Exalted: فَبِأَيِّ آلَاءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبانِ — "So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny?" Bounties (الآلاء) is the plural of ila, meaning blessing (النعمة).

The address in the verse is to the entirety of the two weighty races: the jinn and mankind. This is indicated by the explicit direction of the address to them in what follows, such as His saying: سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ أَيُّهَ الثَّقَلانِ — "We will soon attend to you, O you two weighty ones!" and His saying: يَا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ — "O company of jinn and humans!" etc., and His saying: يُرْسَلُ عَلَيْكُمَا شُوَاظٌ — "There will be unleashed upon you both a flame..." etc. Therefore, no heed should be paid to the one who says the address in the verse is to the male and female of the children of Adam, nor to the one who says it is an address to one using the dual form to signify repetition, like saying "O guard, strike [dual] his neck," meaning "strike his neck, strike his neck."

Directing the address to the two worlds of jinn and mankind is what justifies counting the hardships of the Day of Resurrection and the punishments of the criminals among the people of the Fire—which we shall mention—as being among His bounties and blessings, the Exalted. For driving the evildoers and the people of wretchedness within the system of the universe toward what their wretchedness necessitates and their being recompensed with the consequences of their deeds is among the requirements for the integrity of the general system prevailing in the whole and governing all. Thus, it is a blessing in relation to the whole, even if it is a vengeance in relation to a specific group among them, namely the criminals. This is analogous to what we find in the norms and laws prevailing in societies; for the tightening of control over the people of rebellion and corruption is something upon which the life and survival of society depend, and it is not only the people of righteousness who benefit from it, just as rewarding the people of righteousness with beautiful praise and goodly reward is likewise.

Thus, what is in the Fire of torment and punishment for its people, and what is in the Garden of honor and reward, are bounties and blessings upon the company of jinn and mankind, just as the sun, the moon, the raised heaven, the set earth, the herb, the tree, and others are bounties and blessings upon the people of the world.

It appears from the verse that the jinn have a share of enjoyment in these blessings enumerated throughout the verses, just as mankind does; otherwise, it would not be correct to include them with mankind in the reproach.

His saying, the Exalted: خَلَقَ الْإِنْسَانَ مِنْ صَلْصَالٍ كَالْفَخَّارِ — "He created man out of dry clay like pottery." Dry clay (الصلصال) is dry mud that produces a sound when trodden upon, and pottery (الفخار) is baked clay.

What is meant by "man" is the species, and the meaning of his creation from dry clay like pottery is the ultimate point to which his creation traces back. It has also been said that "man" refers to Adam (peace be upon him).

His saying, the Exalted: وَخَلَقَ الْجَانَّ مِنْ مَارِجٍ مِنْ نَارٍ — "And He created the jinn from a flame of fire." Flame (المارج) is the pure flame of fire; it has also been said to be a flame mixed with blackness. The discussion regarding the jinn (الجان) is like the discussion regarding man; what is meant is the species of jinn. They are considered created from fire in view of the ultimate point to which their creation traces back. It has also been said that "the jinn" refers to the father of the jinn.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ وَرَبُّ الْمَغْرِبَيْنِ — "Lord of the two easts and Lord of the two wests." What is meant by the two easts is the sunrise of summer and the sunrise of winter. Through this, the four seasons occur and provisions are ordered. It has also been said that the two easts refer to the rising of the sun and the moon, and the two wests to their setting.

His saying, the Exalted: مَرَجَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ يَلْتَقِيَانِ بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخٌ لَا يَبْغِيَانِ — "He merged the two seas, meeting each other. Between them is a barrier they do not overstep." Merging (المرج) is mixing, and it also means releasing. It is said: "He merged it," meaning he mixed it, or "He merged it," meaning he released it; the first meaning is more apparent. It appears that what is meant by the two seas is the sweet, fresh water and the salty, bitter water. The Exalted said: وَمَا يَسْتَوِي الْبَحْرَانِ هَذَا عَذْبٌ فُرَاتٌ سَائِغٌ شَرَابُهُ وَهَذَا مِلْحٌ أُجَاجٌ وَمِنْ كُلٍّ تَأْكُلُونَ لَحْمًا طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُونَ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَهَا — "The two seas are not equal: one is fresh, sweet and pleasant to drink, and the other is salt and bitter. From each you eat fresh meat, and extract ornaments which you wear" (35:12).

The best of what has been said regarding the two verses is that the two seas refer to the genus of the salty sea, which covers nearly three-quarters of the earth's sphere—including the oceans and non-oceans—and the sweet sea stored in the earth's reservoirs, from which the earth bursts forth so that springs and large rivers flow and pour into the salty sea. They continue to meet, yet between them is a barrier (حاجز), which is the very earth reservoirs and channels that prevent the salty sea from overstepping (يبغي) the sweet sea, overwhelming it and turning it into a salty sea, thereby destroying life. Likewise, it prevents the sweet sea from increasing its flow into the salty sea and turning it into sweet water, thereby destroying the benefit of its saltiness in purifying the air and other things.

The salty sea continues to supply the sweet sea with rain, which the clouds take from it and rain upon the earth to be stored in the earth's reservoirs, and the sweet sea supplies the salty sea by pouring into it.

So the meaning of the two verses—and Allah knows best—is the mixing of the two seas, the sweet and fresh and the salty and bitter, while they are continuous in their meeting, with a barrier between them so they do not transgress by one overwhelming the other and removing its quality of sweetness or saltiness, which would disrupt the system of life and survival.

His saying, the Exalted: يَخْرُجُ مِنْهُمَا اللُّؤْلُؤُ وَالْمَرْجَانُ — "From them both come out the pearl and the coral." That is, from both the sweet and salty seas together, and that is among their benefits from which man profits. The discussion regarding this has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: وَمَا يَسْتَوِي الْبَحْرَانِ (35:12).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَهُ الْجَوَارِ الْمُنْشَآتُ فِي الْبَحْرِ كَالْأَعْلَامِ — "His are the ships [sailing] with raised sails in the sea like mountains." Ships (الجواري) is the plural of jariyah, meaning a vessel. Raised (المنشئات) is the passive participle of insha', which is to originate and nurture a thing. Mountains (الأعلام) is the plural of ‘alam (with two fathas), meaning a mountain.

The ships are considered owned by Him, the Exalted, despite being made by man, because the causes acting in their origination—the wood, iron, and other parts from which they are composed—and the man who assembles them, along with his consciousness, thought, and will, are all created by Him and owned by Him; thus, what their work produces is His property.

He, the Exalted, is the one who bestowed them as a blessing upon man, inspired him with the way to make them, the benefits resulting from them, and the means to utilize their vast advantages.

His saying, the Exalted: كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍ وَيَبْقَى وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو الْجَلَالِ وَالْإِكْرَامِ — "Everyone on it is ephemeral, and there endures the Face of your Lord, the Possessor of Majesty and Generosity." The pronoun in "on it" (عَلَيْهَا) refers to the earth; that is, every possessor of consciousness and intellect on the earth shall perish. In this is a recording of the cessation and disappearance of the two weighty races.

He used the term denoting those possessed of intellect—"everyone on it" (مَنْ)—and did not say "everything on it" (مَا) because the discourse in the Surah is arranged to enumerate His blessings and bounties, the Exalted, for the two weighty races in their two stages of existence, the world and the Hereafter.

The indication of His saying "ephemeral" (فَانٍ) toward the future, as is also understood from the context, implies that His saying "Everyone on it is ephemeral" refers to the termination of the period of the worldly life and the lifting of its rule by the perishing of those upon it—namely the two weighty races—and the dawning of the other life upon them. Both of these—the perishing of those upon it and the dawning of the life of recompense upon them—are among the blessings and bounties, because the worldly life is a preparatory life for the purpose of the Hereafter, and the transition from the preparation to the purpose and goal is a blessing.

By this, the argument of those who say "What blessing is there in perishing such that it is made among the blessings and counted among the bounties?" is refuted.

The summary of the answer is that the reality of this perishing is the return to Allah by transitioning from the world, as explained by many verses in His speech, the Exalted; it is not absolute annihilation.

His saying: وَيَبْقَى وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ — "and there endures the Face of your Lord." The face (وجه) of a thing is that by which it faces another and by which another intends it. In relation to Him, the Glorified, it refers to His noble attributes that mediate between Him and His creation, through which blessings of creation and governance descend upon them—such as knowledge, power, hearing, sight, mercy, forgiveness, and provision. A detailed discussion has already preceded in the commentary on Surah al-A‘raf regarding His names and attributes, the Exalted, being intermediaries between Him and His creation.

His saying: ذُو الْجَلَالِ وَالْإِكْرَامِ — "the Possessor of Majesty and Generosity." In majesty (الجلال) there is something of the meaning of loftiness and spiritual transcendence over others; thus, it suits those attributes containing a sense of repelling and preventing, such as highness, transcendence, greatness, pride, supremacy, encompassment, might, and dominance.

There remains for generosity (الإكرام) the meaning containing the description of splendor and beauty that attracts others and enamors them, such as knowledge, power, life, mercy, bounty, beauty, and goodness. These are called attributes of beauty (صفات الجمال), just as the first category is called attributes of majesty (صفات الجلال). The names are also called, according to the attributes of beauty or majesty they contain, names of beauty or majesty.

Thus, "Possessor of Majesty and Generosity" is one of the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) whose concept encompasses all the names of beauty and majesty together.

The one named by it in reality is the Sacred Essence, as in His saying at the end of the Surah: تَبَارَكَ اسْمُ رَبِّكَ ذِي الْجَلَالِ وَالْإِكْرَامِ — "Blessed is the Name of your Lord, the Possessor of Majesty and Generosity." However, in this verse—وَيَبْقَى وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو الْجَلَالِ وَالْإِكْرَامِ—it is applied to the "Face." This is either because it is an attribute detached from its adjectival function for the sake of praise—the implied meaning being "He is the Possessor of Majesty and Generosity"—or because what is meant by the "Face," as previously mentioned, is His noble attribute and His sacred name, and applying the name to the name ultimately results in applying the name to the Essence.

The meaning of the verse—on the assumption that the "Face" refers to that by which a thing faces another, which is the Name (and it is known that the endurance of the Name (1) is a branch of the endurance of the Named)—is: and your Lord, mighty is His Name, endures with His majesty and generosity, without their perishing having any effect upon Him or changing anything of Him.

On the assumption that the "Face" refers to that which others intend of Him—and its instance is everything attributed to Him, the Exalted, such that it is intended by the one turning toward Him, like His prophets, His friends, His religion, His reward, His proximity, and other such things—the meaning is: and there endures after the perishing of the people of the world that which is with Him, the Exalted, and is from His side and direction, such as the types of recompense, reward, and proximity to Him. The Exalted said: مَا عِنْدَكُمْ يَنْفَدُ وَمَا عِنْدَ اللهِ بَاقٍ — "What is with you will be exhausted, but what is with Allah will remain" (16:96).

Some discussion has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هَالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ — "Everything is to perish except His Face" (28:88), which is not without benefit in this context.

His saying, the Exalted: يَسْأَلُهُ مَنْ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِي شَأْنٍ — "Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him. Every day He is engaged in some work." Their asking is an asking of need; they are in need of Him from every aspect, their existences are dependent upon Him, and they cling to the hem of His self-sufficiency and bounty. The Exalted said: أَنْتُمُ الْفُقَرَاءُ إِلَى اللهِ وَاللهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ — "It is you who are the needy in relation to Allah, and Allah—He is the All-sufficient" (35:15). Regarding this meaning of asking, He said: وَآتَاكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ مَا سَأَلْتُمُوهُ — "And He gave you of all that you asked Him" (14:34).

His saying: كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِي شَأْنٍ — "Every day He is engaged in some work." The indefiniteness of "work" (شَأْنٍ) indicates diversity and difference. The meaning is: every day He, the Exalted, is in a work different from the work of the day before it and the day after it. No act of His acts is repeated twice, nor does one of His works resemble another work from all aspects. Rather, He acts without a prior model, which is origination (الإبداع). The Exalted said: بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "the Originator of the heavens and the earth" (2:117).

The meaning of the day being a container (ظرفية اليوم) is His encompassment, the Glorified, in the station of action over all things. Thus, He, the Glorified, is in every time yet not in time, in every place yet not in place, and with every thing yet not adjacent to any thing.

(1) What is meant by the Name is that which the verbal name signifies, not the signifying word itself.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, Muhammad ibn al-Munkadir narrated from Jabir ibn Abdillah, who said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited al-Rahman to the people, they remained silent and said nothing. So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The jinn were better in response than you. Whenever I recited to them فَبِأَيِّ آلَاءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ, they said: "No, nor do we deny any of the bounties of our Lord."'"

I say: This meaning is also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several compilers of hadith—and he authenticated it—from Ibn Umar, from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In al-Uyun, with its chain to Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him): regarding what the Syrian asked Ali (peace be upon him), it includes: "He asked him about the name of the father of the jinn, and he said: 'Shuman, and he is the one who was created from a flame of fire.'"

In al-Ihtijaj, from Ali (peace be upon him) in a hadith: "As for His saying: رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ وَرَبُّ الْمَغْرِبَيْنِ, the sunrise of winter is distinct and the sunrise of summer is distinct. Do you not recognize that from the proximity and distance of the sun?"

I say: al-Qummi narrated this meaning in his tafsir as a mursal and mudmar narration.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Abbas: regarding His saying: مَرَجَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ يَلْتَقِيَانِ, he said: "Ali and Fatimah." بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخٌ لَا يَبْغِيَانِ, he said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)." يَخْرُجُ مِنْهُمَا اللُّؤْلُؤُ وَالْمَرْجَانُ, he said: "al-Hasan and al-Husayn."

I say: It is also narrated by Ibn Mardawayh from Anas ibn Malik similarly, and it is narrated in al-Majma‘ al-Bayan from Salman al-Farsi, Sa‘id ibn Jubayr, and Sufyan al-Thawri. This is from the inner meaning (البطن).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍ, he said: "Those on the face of the earth." وَيَبْقَى وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ, he said: "The religion of your Lord." Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) said: "We are the Face through which Allah is approached."

In the Manaqib of Ibn Shahr Ashub, regarding His saying: وَيَبْقَى وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "We are the Face of Allah."

I say: There are other narrations with the meaning of these two, and the explanation for interpreting the "Face" as the religion or the Imam has already preceded.

In al-Kafi, in a sermon by Ali (peace be upon him): "Praise be to Allah who does not die and whose wonders do not cease—because every day He is in a work of a new origination that was not."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: regarding the verse, he said: "He gives life and causes death, and He increases and decreases."

In al-Majma‘, from Abu al-Darda, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): regarding His saying: كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِي شَأْنٍ, he said: "Of His work is that He forgives a sin, relieves a distress, raises a people, and humbles others."

I say: It is narrated from him in al-Durr al-Manthur, and what is in its meaning is narrated from Ibn Umar from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and its wording is: "He forgives a sin and relieves a distress."

Ayahs

سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ أَيُّهَ ٱلثَّقَلَانِ (٣١)
Soon We shall make Ourselves unoccupied for you, O you notable two! (31)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٢)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (32)
يَـٰمَعْشَرَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ إِنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَن تَنفُذُوا۟ مِنْ أَقْطَارِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنفُذُوا۟ ۚ لَا تَنفُذُونَ إِلَّا بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ (٣٣)
O company of jinn and humans! If you can pass through the confines of the heavens and the earth, then do pass through. But you will not pass through except by an authority [from Allah]. (33)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٤)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (34)
يُرْسَلُ عَلَيْكُمَا شُوَاظٌ مِّن نَّارٍ وَنُحَاسٌ فَلَا تَنتَصِرَانِ (٣٥)
There will be unleashed upon you a flash of fire and a smoke; then you will not be able to help one another. (35)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٦)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (36)
فَإِذَا ٱنشَقَّتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ فَكَانَتْ وَرْدَةً كَٱلدِّهَانِ (٣٧)
When the sky is split open, and turns crimson like tanned leather. (37)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٨)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (38)
فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يُسْـَٔلُ عَن ذَنۢبِهِۦٓ إِنسٌ وَلَا جَآنٌّ (٣٩)
On that day neither humans will be questioned about their sins nor jinn. (39)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٠)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (40)
يُعْرَفُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ فَيُؤْخَذُ بِٱلنَّوَٰصِى وَٱلْأَقْدَامِ (٤١)
The guilty will be recognized by their mark; so they will be seized by their forelocks and feet. (41)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٢)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (42)
هَـٰذِهِۦ جَهَنَّمُ ٱلَّتِى يُكَذِّبُ بِهَا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٤٣)
‘This is the hell which the guilty would deny!’ (43)
يَطُوفُونَ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَ حَمِيمٍ ءَانٍ (٤٤)
They shall circuit between it and boiling hot water. (44)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٥)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (45)
وَلِمَنْ خَافَ مَقَامَ رَبِّهِۦ جَنَّتَانِ (٤٦)
For him who stands in awe of his Lord will be two gardens. (46)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٧)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (47)
ذَوَاتَآ أَفْنَانٍ (٤٨)
Both abounding in branches. (48)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٩)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (49)
فِيهِمَا عَيْنَانِ تَجْرِيَانِ (٥٠)
In both of them will be two flowing springs. (50)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥١)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (51)
فِيهِمَا مِن كُلِّ فَـٰكِهَةٍ زَوْجَانِ (٥٢)
In both of them will be two kinds of every fruit. (52)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥٣)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (53)
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ فُرُشٍۭ بَطَآئِنُهَا مِنْ إِسْتَبْرَقٍ ۚ وَجَنَى ٱلْجَنَّتَيْنِ دَانٍ (٥٤)
[They will be] reclining on beds lined with green silk. And the fruit of the two gardens will be near at hand. (54)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥٥)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (55)
فِيهِنَّ قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَآنٌّ (٥٦)
In them are maidens of restrained glances, whom no human has touched before, nor jinn. (56)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥٧)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (57)
كَأَنَّهُنَّ ٱلْيَاقُوتُ وَٱلْمَرْجَانُ (٥٨)
As though they were rubies and corals. (58)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥٩)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (59)
هَلْ جَزَآءُ ٱلْإِحْسَـٰنِ إِلَّا ٱلْإِحْسَـٰنُ (٦٠)
Is the requital of goodness anything but goodness? (60)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦١)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (61)
وَمِن دُونِهِمَا جَنَّتَانِ (٦٢)
Beside these two, there will be two [other] gardens. (62)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٣)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (63)
مُدْهَآمَّتَانِ (٦٤)
Dark green. (64)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٥)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (65)
فِيهِمَا عَيْنَانِ نَضَّاخَتَانِ (٦٦)
In both of them will be two gushing springs. (66)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٧)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (67)
فِيهِمَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ وَنَخْلٌ وَرُمَّانٌ (٦٨)
In both of them will be fruits, date-palms and pomegranates. (68)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٩)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (69)
فِيهِنَّ خَيْرَٰتٌ حِسَانٌ (٧٠)
In them are maidens good and lovely. (70)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٧١)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (71)
حُورٌ مَّقْصُورَٰتٌ فِى ٱلْخِيَامِ (٧٢)
Houris secluded in pavilions. (72)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٧٣)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (73)
لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَآنٌّ (٧٤)
Whom no human has touched before, nor jinn. (74)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٧٥)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (75)
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ رَفْرَفٍ خُضْرٍ وَعَبْقَرِىٍّ حِسَانٍ (٧٦)
Reclining on green cushions and lovely carpets. (76)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٧٧)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (77)
تَبَـٰرَكَ ٱسْمُ رَبِّكَ ذِى ٱلْجَلَـٰلِ وَٱلْإِكْرَامِ (٧٨)
Blessed is the Name of your Lord, the Majestic and the Munificent! (78)

(Commentary)

This is the second section of the verses of this Surah. It describes the second stage of the existence of the two heavy weights (الثقلان) — the jinn and mankind — which is the stage of returning to Allah and the recompense for deeds. It enumerates the favors of Allah (آلاء الله) upon them, just as the preceding verses formed the first section describing the first stage of existence and enumerating Allah’s favors therein.

His saying, the Exalted: سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ أَيُّهَ الثَّقَلانِ — We will soon attend to you, O you two heavy weights! (55:31) It is said: "So-and-so became free (فرغ) for such-and-such a matter" when he was previously occupied with other affairs, then left them and restricted his engagement to that specific matter out of concern for it.

Thus, the meaning of "We will soon attend to you (سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ)" is: We shall fold up the carpet of the first life and occupy Ourselves with you. The subsequent verses clarify that this "occupation" with them refers to their resurrection, their reckoning, and the recompense for their deeds, whether good or evil. Therefore, "attending to them" (الفراغ لهم) is a metaphor (استعارة) by way of metonymy (الكناية) for the transition of the stages of existence.

This "attending to them" does not contradict the fact that no task distracts Him, the Exalted, from another task (لا يشغله شأن عن شأن). For the mentioned "attending" looks toward the transition of the stage of existence, while the fact that no task distracts Him looks toward the absolute nature and vastness of His power (القدرة). Similarly, the fact that He, the Exalted, is every day in a new state (كل يوم هو في شأن) — which looks toward the diversity of states — does not contradict the fact that no task distracts Him from another.

The "two heavy weights" (الثقلان) are the jinn and mankind. The plural pronouns in "to you" (لَكُمْ) and "if you can" (إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ) and others refer to them because they are two groups comprising many individuals.

His saying, the Exalted: يا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَنْ تَنْفُذُوا مِنْ أَقْطارِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ فَانْفُذُوا — O company of jinn and humans! If you can pass through the confines of the heavens and the earth, then do pass! (55:33) etc. This address — as indicated by the context — is one of the addresses of the Day of Resurrection, and it is a challenge intended to demonstrate incapacity (خطاب تعجيزي).

What is meant by "ability" (الاستطاعة) is power (القدرة), and by "passing through" (النفوذ) the confines is fleeing. "Confines" (الأقطار) is the plural of qutr (قطر), which means a side or region (الناحية).

The meaning is: O company of jinn and humans — and the jinn are mentioned first because they are more capable of swift movement — if you have the power to flee by passing through the regions of the heavens and the earth, exiting the kingdom of Allah and escaping His reckoning, then flee and pass through.

His saying: لا تَنْفُذُونَ إِلَّا بِسُلْطانٍ — But you will not pass through except by [His] authority (55:33) means: you are not capable of passing through except by a type of authority (سلطة) over that, and you possess none. Authority (السلطان) here refers to existential power (القدرة الوجودية); it can also mean a proof (البرهان), an absolute argument (الحجة), or sovereignty (الملك).

It has been said that the "passing through" denied in the verse refers to scientific penetration (النفوذ العلمي) into the heavens and the earth from their regions, but you have seen that the context does not support this.

His saying, the Exalted: يُرْسَلُ عَلَيْكُما شُواظٌ مِنْ نارٍ وَنُحاسٌ فَلا تَنْتَصِرانِ — There will be unleashed upon you a flame of fire and a [molten] copper, and you will not be able to help each other (55:35). The term shuwādh (الشواظ) — as mentioned by al-Raghib — is a flame without smoke. Close to this is what is in al-Majma‘: that it is the green flame that detaches from the fire. Nuhās (النحاس) is smoke; al-Raghib said it is a flame without smoke. The meaning is clear.

His saying: فَلا تَنْتَصِرانِ — and you will not be able to help each other (55:35) means you will not assist one another such that some of you help others to remove the affliction or escape the hardship, for the efficacy of causes (الأسباب) will have ceased, and there is no protector today from Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذَا انْشَقَّتِ السَّماءُ فَكانَتْ وَرْدَةً كَالدِّهانِ — When the sky is split open and turns rosy like red hide (55:37) meaning it becomes red like dihān (الدهان), which is red leather (الأديم الأحمر).

His saying, the Exalted: فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لا يُسْئَلُ عَنْ ذَنْبِهِ إِنْسٌ وَلا جَانٌ — On that day, neither human nor jinn will be questioned about his sin (55:39). This verse and those following it until the end of the Surah describe the reckoning and the recompense; they describe the state of the guilty (المجرمين) and those who fear the station of their Lord, and what they shall ultimately reach.

Furthermore, the verse describes the speed of the reckoning, and He, the Exalted, has said: وَاللهُ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ — and Allah is swift at reckoning (24:39).

What is meant by "that day" is the Day of Resurrection. The questioning that is denied is the familiar type of questioning. The denial of questioning in this verse does not contradict its affirmation in His saying: وَقِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ مَسْؤُلُونَ — [And say,] ‘Stop them, for they are to be questioned’ (37:24) and His saying: فَوَ رَبِّكَ لَنَسْئَلَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — By your Lord, We will question them all (16:92). This is because that Day has different stations (مواقف): in some they are questioned, in some their mouths are sealed and their limbs speak, and in some they are recognized by their marks (السيماء).

His saying, the Exalted: يُعْرَفُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيماهُمْ فَيُؤْخَذُ بِالنَّواصِي وَالْأَقْدامِ — The guilty will be recognized by their marks; so they will be seized by their forelocks and feet (55:41). This is in the position of answering an implied question, as if it were asked: "If they are not questioned about their sins, what will be done with them?" The answer is that the guilty will be recognized by their marks, etc. For this reason, the sentence is independent and not joined by a conjunction. What is meant by their "marks" (سيماهم) is the prominent signs on their faces.

His saying: فَيُؤْخَذُ بِالنَّواصِي وَالْأَقْدامِ — so they will be seized by their forelocks and feet (55:41) is a statement branching from the aforementioned recognition. "Forelocks" (النواصي) is the plural of nāsiyah (ناصية), which is the hair on the front of the head. "Feet" (الأقدام) is the plural of qadam (قدم). The phrase "by the forelocks" (بِالنَّواصِي) acts as the pro-agent (نائب فاعل) of "seized" (يؤخذ).

The meaning is: No one will be questioned about his sin; rather, the guilty will be recognized by the signs apparent on their faces, and the guilty will be seized by their forelocks and feet and cast into the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: هذِهِ جَهَنَّمُ الَّتِي يُكَذِّبُ بِهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ — This is the hell that the guilty used to deny (55:43) until His saying: آنٍ — boiling (55:44). This is the content of an implied speech, meaning: it will be said on that day, "This is the hell that the guilty used to deny." Al-Tabarsi said: "It is possible that when Allah, the Glorified, informed that they would be seized by the forelocks and feet, He said to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): 'This is the hell that the guilty among your people used to deny; they shall soon enter it, so let their affair not weigh heavily upon you.'" End quote.

Hamīm (الحميم) is hot water, and ān (الآني) is that which has reached its ultimate heat. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ — For him who fears the standing before his Lord, there will be two gardens (55:46). This is the beginning of the description of the state of the felicitous among those who fear the station of their Lord. "Station" (المقام) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) meaning "standing" (القيام), annexed to its agent. What is meant is His, the Exalted, standing over him regarding his deeds—which is His encompassment (إحاطته) and knowledge of what he has done, His recording of it, and His recompense for it. He, the Exalted, said: أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قائِمٌ عَلى كُلِّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ — Is He who stands over every soul for what it has earned [like him who does not]? (13:33).

It is also possible that "station" (المقام) is a noun of place (اسم مكان), and the annexation is possessive. What is meant is His station and position, the Exalted, in relation to His servant—namely, that He, the Exalted, is his Lord who manages his affairs. Part of managing his affairs is that He called him through the tongues of His messengers to faith and righteous deeds, and decreed that He would recompense him for what he did, whether good or evil. This is while He encompasses him, is with him, is All-Hearing of what he says, All-Seeing of what he does, and is All-Attentive (لطيف) and All-Aware (خبير).

Fear of Allah, the Exalted, might be fear of His punishment for disbelief in Him and disobedience to Him. The implication of this is that the worship of one who worships Him out of fear in this sense is intended to escape punishment, not purely for the sake of Allah. This is the "worship of slaves" (عبادة العبيد) who worship their masters out of fear of discipline. Similarly, the worship of one who worships Him out of greed for reward has as its goal the attainment of what the soul desires, rather than His Noble Face; this is the "worship of merchants" (عبادة التجار), as mentioned in the narrations, some of which have been cited previously.

The fear mentioned in the verse — وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ — is apparently different from this fear. For this is fear of punishment, which is distinct from fear of His, the Exalted, standing over His servant regarding what he has done, or fear of His station in relation to His servant. It is a specific state of being affected (تأثر خاص) experienced by one who possesses nothing but smallness and insignificance in the presence of the court of Grandeur and Majesty (العظمة والكبرياء), and the manifestation of the effect of lowliness, humility, and annihilation (الاندكاك) before the absolute Might and Omnipotence (العزة والجبروت).

Worshipping Him, the Exalted, out of fear of Him in this sense is a form of submission (خضوع) to Him because He is Allah, the Possessor of Majesty and Generosity (ذو الجلال والإكرام), not out of fear of His punishment nor greed for His reward. Rather, it involves sincerity (إخلاص) of action for His Noble Face. This meaning of fear is that with which Allah described His honored angels, who are infallible (معصومون) and secure from the punishment of opposition and the consequence of disobedience. He, the Exalted, said: يَخافُونَ رَبَّهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ — They fear their Lord [who is] above them (16:50).

Thus, it becomes clear from what has preceded that those referred to by His saying: "And for him who fears (وَلِمَنْ خافَ)" are the people of sincerity (أهل الإخلاص) who are submissive to His Majesty, the Exalted, worshipping Him because He is Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), not out of fear of His punishment nor greed for His reward. It is not unlikely that they are the ones named "the foremost" (سابقين) in His saying: وَكُنْتُمْ أَزْواجاً ثَلاثَةً — and you will be three groups (56:7) until He said: وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones; they are the ones brought near [to Allah] (56:10-11).

Regarding His saying: "two gardens" (جَنَّتانِ), it has been said: one of them is his residence and the place where his beloved ones visit him, and the other is the residence of his spouses and servants. It has also been said: two orchards, one orchard inside his palace and one orchard outside it. It has been said: two residences between which he moves to complete his pleasure. It has been said: a garden for his belief and a garden for his deeds. It has been said: a garden for performing acts of obedience and a garden for abandoning acts of disobedience. It has been said: a physical garden and a spiritual garden. These views — as you can see — have no evidence for any of them.

It has also been said: a garden as a reward and a garden as a favor (تفضل) bestowed upon him. This might be sensed from His saying, the Exalted: لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ — They will have therein whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more (50:35), as per what was mentioned in its commentary.

His saying, the Exalted: ذَواتا أَفْنانٍ — abounding in branches (55:48). Dhawātā (ذَواتا) is the dual of dhāt (ذات). Afnān (أَفْنانٍ) is either the plural of fann (فن) meaning "type" (النوع), so the meaning is: possessing types of fruits and the like; or it is the plural of fanan (فنن) meaning a fresh, supple branch (الغصن الرطب اللين), so the meaning is: possessing supple branches on their trees.

His saying, the Exalted: فِيهِما عَيْنانِ تَجْرِيانِ — In both of them are two flowing springs (55:50). The two springs are left indefinite, which indicates the magnificence of their state.

His saying, the Exalted: فِيهِما مِنْ كُلِّ فاكِهَةٍ زَوْجانِ — In both of them are two kinds of every fruit (55:52) meaning two types. It has been said: a type known to them which they saw in the world, and a type unknown which they did not see in the world. Others have said otherwise, but there is no indication in the speech for any of that.

His saying, the Exalted: مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلى فُرُشٍ بَطائِنُها مِنْ إِسْتَبْرَقٍ — Reclining on couches lined with brocade (55:54) etc. Furush (الفرش) is the plural of firāsh (فراش) — couch or bedding. Batā’in (البطائن) is the plural of bitānah (بطانة), which is the interior or inside of a thing, as opposed to dhahā’ir (الظهائر), the plural of dhihārah (ظهارة) — the exterior. Istabraq (الإستبرق) is thick silk. It is said in al-Majma‘: "He mentioned the lining (البطانة) and did not mention the exterior (الظهارة) because the lining indicates that it has an exterior, and the lining is inferior to the exterior; thus it indicates that the exterior is superior to brocade." End quote.

His saying: وَجَنَى الْجَنَّتَيْنِ دانٍ — and the fruit of both gardens is within reach (55:54). Janā (الجنى) is the fruit that is gathered, and dān (دانٍ) is the active participle from dunuww (الدنو) meaning "closeness" (القرب); i.e., what is gathered from the fruits of the two gardens is near.

His saying, the Exalted: فِيهِنَّ قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ — In them are maidens of restrained glances (55:56) until the end of the verse. The pronoun in "in them" (فِيهِنَّ) refers to the couches (الفرش), though it has been allowed to refer to the gardens (الجنان), for they are gardens such that for every one of the friends of Allah there are two gardens. Tarf (الطرف) is the eyelid. What is meant by "restrained glances" is that they are content with their spouses and desire no one else.

His saying: لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنْسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلا جَانٌ — whom no human has touched before them, nor any jinn (55:56). Tamt (الطمث) is defloration and intercourse involving blood. The meaning is: no human or jinn has touched them through intercourse before their spouses.

His saying, the Exalted: كَأَنَّهُنَّ الْياقُوتُ وَالْمَرْجانُ — as if they were rubies and corals (55:58) meaning in the purity of color, splendor, and radiance.

His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ جَزاءُ الْإِحْسانِ إِلَّا الْإِحْسانُ — Is the recompense of goodness anything but goodness? (55:60). This is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for what has been mentioned of His, the Exalted, goodness toward them with the two gardens and the types of blessings and favors therein. It signifies that He, the Exalted, bestows this goodness upon them as a recompense for their goodness in fearing the station of their Lord.

The verse indicates that what they are given of the Garden and its bliss is a recompense for their deeds. As for what is understood from some verses that they are given a favor (فضلا) beyond the recompense for their deeds, these verses do not address that, unless it is said: goodness (الإحسان) is only complete when it exceeds what the benefactor was given. Thus, the absolute use of "goodness" in His saying: "except goodness (إِلَّا الْإِحْسانُ)" implies an increase.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْ دُونِهِما جَنَّتانِ — And besides these two there are two [other] gardens (55:62). The dual pronoun refers to the two gardens described in the previous verses. The meaning of "besides these two" (مِنْ دُونِهِما) is: lower in degree and less in merit and honor than them, even if they are similar to the two previous gardens in their blessings and favors. It has already been mentioned that the two previous gardens are for the people of sincerity who fear the station of their Lord; thus, these two gardens are for those below them among the believers who worship Allah, the Glorified, out of fear of the Fire or greed for the Garden, and they are the "Companions of the Right" (أصحاب اليمين).

It has also been said: the meaning of "besides these two" is "near to them." In that case, it is understood from the context that these two gardens are also for the people of the two gardens mentioned before. Some have even claimed that these two gardens are better than the previous two and that the attributes mentioned in them are more praiseworthy.

However, by reflecting on what we have presented regarding the meaning of "for him who fears the station of his Lord," and what is understood from His, the Exalted, speech that the people of the Garden are of two categories: the "brought near" (المقربون) who are the people of sincerity, and the "Companions of the Right," you will recognize the strength of the first view.

His saying, the Exalted: مُدْهامَّتانِ — of a deep green (55:64). Idhihmām (الادهيمام) comes from duhmah (الدهمة), which is the intensity of greenness such that it tends toward blackness, and it refers to the vibrancy of the tree.

His saying, the Exalted: فِيهِما عَيْنانِ نَضَّاخَتانِ — In both of them are two gushing springs (55:66) meaning they are erupting, coming out of their source with force.

His saying, the Exalted: فِيهِما فاكِهَةٌ وَنَخْلٌ وَرُمَّانٌ — In both of them are fruits, date-palms and pomegranates (55:68). What is meant by "fruits" and "pomegranates" is their trees, by the context of "date-palms."

His saying, the Exalted: فِيهِنَّ خَيْراتٌ حِسانٌ — In them are maidens, good and beautiful (55:70). The pronoun in "in them" (فِيهِنَّ) refers to the gardens (الجنان) considering that they are two gardens from these two gardens. It has been said the pronoun refers to the four gardens mentioned in the verses, and it has been said the pronoun refers to the fruits, date-palms, and pomegranates.

"Good" (الخير) is most often used for internal qualities (المعاني), just as "beautiful" (الحسن) is most often used for external forms (الصور). Based on this, the meaning of "good and beautiful" is that they are good in their character and beautiful in their faces.

His saying, the Exalted: حُورٌ مَقْصُوراتٌ فِي الْخِيامِ — houris, secluded in pavilions (55:72). Khiyām (الخيام) is the plural of khaymah (خيمة), which is a tent (الفسطاط). Their being "secluded in pavilions" means they are protected and not common, with no share in them for anyone other than their spouses.

His saying, the Exalted: لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنْسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلا جَانٌ — whom no human has touched before them, nor any jinn (55:74). Its meaning has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلى رَفْرَفٍ خُضْرٍ وَعَبْقَرِيٍّ حِسانٍ — Reclining on green cushions and beautiful carpets (55:76). In al-Sihāh: Rafraf (الرفرف) are green cloths from which sitting places are made. End quote. It has been said they are cushions (الوسائد), and other things have been said. Khudr (الخضر) is the plural of akhdar (أخضر) — green — an attribute of rafraf. ‘Abqarī (العبقري) has been said to be carpets (الزرابي), or rugs (الطنافس), or embroidered cloths (الثياب الموشاة), or silk brocade (الديباج).

His saying, the Exalted: تَبارَكَ اسْمُ رَبِّكَ ذِي الْجَلالِ وَالْإِكْرامِ — Blessed is the Name of your Lord, the Possessor of Majesty and Generosity (55:78). This is a beautiful praise for Him, the Exalted, for the fact that both stages of existence — this world and the Hereafter — are filled with His blessings, favors, and benedictions descending from Him by His vast mercy. By this, it becomes clear that what is meant by His blessed Name is "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) with which the Surah opened. Tabārak (التبارك) is the abundance of goodness and blessings that proceed [from Him].

Thus, His saying: "Blessed is the Name of your Lord" means: Blessed is Allah, who is named "the All-Beneficent," for He has poured forth these favors.

His saying: "the Possessor of Majesty and Generosity" (ذِي الْجلالِ وَالْإِكْرامِ) is an allusion to His being named by His Beautiful Names (أسماؤه الحسنى) and His being described by the descriptive meanings they indicate, and the attributes of Majesty (الجلال) — the attributes of transcendence and awe — and Beauty (الجمال) — the attributes of grace and mercy. The attributes of the Agent (الفاعل) have a manifestation in His acts and an effect therein by which the act is linked to its Agent. Thus, He, the Exalted, created the creation and organized the system because He is the Originator (بديع), the Creator (خالق), and the Beginner (مبدئ). He perfected the act because He is the All-Knowing (عليم) and All-Wise (حكيم). He recompensed the people of obedience with good because He is the All-Loving (ودود), the All-Appreciative (شكور), the All-Forgiving (غفور), and the Most Merciful (رحيم). And He recompensed the people of transgression with evil because He is the Avenger (منتقم) and Severe in Punishment (شديد العقاب).

Therefore, describing the Lord — whose vast mercy has been praised — as the "Possessor of Majesty and Generosity" is to indicate that His Beautiful Names and His Sublime Attributes have a role in the descent of blessings and goodness from Him, and that His blessings and favors bear the stamp of His Beautiful Names and His Sublime Attributes, Blessed and Exalted is He.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘: It has come in the report: "The creation will be surrounded by angels — and by a tongue of fire — then they will be called: يا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ — O company of jinn and humans! If you can... until His saying: يُرْسَلُ عَلَيْكُما شُواظٌ مِنْ نارٍ — There will be unleashed upon you a flame of fire..."

I say: This meaning is also narrated from Mus‘adah ibn Sadaqah from Kulayb from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In al-Kāfī, with its chain of transmission from Dawud al-Raqqi from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ — For him who fears the standing before his Lord, there will be two gardens (55:46). He said: "من علم أن الله يراه ويسمع ما يقول ـ ويعلم ما يعمله من خير أو شر ـ فيحجزه ذلك عن القبيح من الأعمال ـ فذلك الذي خاف مقام ربه ونهى النفس عن الهوى — Whoever knows that Allah sees him and hears what he says — and knows what he does of good or evil — and that restrains him from ugly deeds — that is the one who fears the station of his Lord and forbids the soul from desire."

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, Ibn Mani‘, al-Hakim in Nawādir al-Usūl, al-Nasa’i, al-Bazzar, Abu Ya‘la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu al-Darda’: that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this verse: وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ — For him who fears the standing before his Lord, there will be two gardens (55:46). I said: "Even if he commits adultery and even if he steals, O Messenger of Allah?" The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said a second time: وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ. I said: "Even if he commits adultery and even if he steals?" He said: "Yes, even if it be against the wishes of Abu al-Darda’."

I say: The narration is not without issues, for fear of His, the Exalted, station does not coexist with these destructive major sins. Indeed, it has been narrated from Abu al-Darda’ himself that which refutes this narration. In al-Durr al-Manthūr, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Yasar, a client of the family of Mu‘awiyah, from Abu al-Darda’: regarding His saying: وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ. He said: It was said: "O Abu al-Darda’, even if he commits adultery and even if he steals?" He replied: "من خاف مقام ربه لم يزن ولم يسرق — Whoever fears the station of his Lord does not commit adultery and does not steal."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ — maidens of restrained glances (55:56). He said: "الحور العين يقصر الطرف عنها من ضوء نورها — The houris: the glance is restrained from them due to the brilliance of their light."

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his father from his grandfather from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): regarding His saying: قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ. He said: "لا ينظرن إلا إلى أزواجهن — They do not look except at their spouses."

In al-Majma‘: regarding His saying, the Exalted: كَأَنَّهُنَّ الْياقُوتُ وَالْمَرْجانُ — as if they were rubies and corals (55:58). In the hadith: "أن المرأة من أهل الجنة يرى مخ ساقها ـ من وراء سبعين حلة من حرير — Indeed, the woman from the people of the Garden: the marrow of her shin is seen from behind seventy robes of silk."

I say: This meaning is reported in several narrations.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyāshī, with its chain of transmission from ‘Ali ibn Salim, who said: I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: "A verse in the Book of Allah is general (مسجلة)." I asked: "And what is it?" He said: "The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: هَلْ جَزاءُ الْإِحْسانِ إِلَّا الْإِحْسانُ — Is the recompense of goodness anything but goodness? (55:60). It applies to the disbeliever and the believer, the righteous and the wicked. Whoever has a favor done for him must reciprocate it, and reciprocation is not just to do as he did until he exceeds it — for if you do just as he did, the merit belongs to him for initiating."

In al-Majma‘: regarding His saying: هَلْ جَزاءُ الْإِحْسانِ إِلَّا الْإِحْسانُ. A narration came from Anas ibn Malik who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this verse and said: "Do you know what your Lord says?" They said: "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said: "Your Lord says: هل جزاء من أنعمنا عليه بالتوحيد إلا الجنة؟ — Is the recompense of one whom We have favored with monotheism (التوحيد) anything but the Garden?"

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding the verse, he said: "ما جزاء من أنعمت عليه بالمعرفة إلا الجنة — What is the recompense of one whom I have favored with gnosis (المعرفة) except the Garden?"

I say: The narration is reported from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). It is attributed in al-Tawhīd to Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his fathers from ‘Ali (peace be upon him) from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) — and its wording is: "أن الله عز وجل قال : ما جزاء من أنعمت عليه بالتوحيد إلا الجنة — Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: 'What is the recompense of one whom I have favored with monotheism except the Garden?'" It is attributed in al-‘Ilal to al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali (peace be upon him) from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) — and the wording is: "هل جزاء من قال : لا إله إلا الله إلا الجنة؟ — Is the recompense of one who says 'There is no god but Allah' anything but the Garden?"

The narration is reported with its various wordings in al-Durr al-Manthūr through different paths from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). His saying: "whom I have favored" is an indication that the servant's goodness is, in reality, a goodness from Allah toward him.

In al-Majma‘: regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْ دُونِهِما جَنَّتانِ — And besides these two there are two [other] gardens (55:62). From al-‘Ala’ ibn Siyabah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): I said to him: "People are surprised at us when we say: 'A group will come out of the Fire and enter the Garden' — they say to us: 'Will they then be with the friends of Allah in the Garden?'" He said: "O ‘Ali, indeed Allah says: وَمِنْ دُونِهِما جَنَّتانِ — And besides these two there are two [other] gardens (55:62); they will not be with the friends of Allah."

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Musa from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): regarding His saying: وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ and His saying: وَمِنْ دُونِهِما جَنَّتانِ. He said: "جنتان من ذهب للمقربين ـ وجنتان من ورق لأصحاب اليمين — Two gardens of gold for the 'brought near' — and two gardens of silver for the 'Companions of the Right'."

I say: The two narrations support what we presented in the commentary of the two verses.

In it, al-Tabarani and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Ayyub who said: I asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about His saying: مُدْهامَّتانِ — of a deep green (55:64). He said: "خضراوان — Two very green ones."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of transmission to Yunus ibn Zabyan from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, the Exalted: نَضَّاخَتانِ — gushing (55:66). He said: "تفوران — Erupting."

In it: regarding His saying: فِيهِنَّ خَيْراتٌ حِسانٌ — In them are maidens, good and beautiful (55:70). He said: "جوار نابتات على شط الكوثر ـ كلما أخذت منها نبتت مكانها أخرى — Maidens growing on the bank of al-Kawthar — whenever you take one of them, another grows in her place."

In al-Majma‘: regarding His saying: خَيْراتٌ حِسانٌ — meaning women of good character and beautiful faces: it was narrated by Umm Salamah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In al-Faqīh, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "الخيرات الحسان من نساء أهل الدنيا ـ وهن أجمل من الحور العين — The 'good and beautiful' are from the women of the people of this world — and they are more beautiful than the houris."

In Rawdat al-Kāfī, with its chain of transmission from al-Halabi who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فِيهِنَّ خَيْراتٌ حِسانٌ. He said: "هن صوالح المؤمنات العارفات — They are the righteous among the believing women who possess gnosis."

I say: There is some ambiguity in the application of the verse to the subject of these two narrations when considering its context.
